judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 2 is more complete: it catches both blocking defects (tenant leak and the exports.py signature break) while Report 1 only catches the tenant leak and misses the required-filters/nightly_export breakage entirely.

report_1.evidence: the WHERE clause contains only status = ... and the full-text match on notes; there is no tenant_id filter, and tenant_id is otherwise unused in the function body except being accepted as a parameter and never applied

report_2.evidence: tenant_id is passed into the function as a parameter but is never referenced in the query — there is no WHERE tenant_id = ... clause at all

The diff changes build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id) to build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id, filters), with filters now a required positional parameter (no default). backend/app/exports.py's nightly_export() still calls build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id) with only two arguments ... the nightly per-tenant CSV export job will raise TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters' on every run once this branch merges

report_1.evidence: I'm not listing this as a merge blocker on its own since I could not construct an actual injection given the enum constraint

report_2.evidence: this isn't currently exploitable for injection through the HTTP endpoint — but it's a latent injection footgun if search_appointments() is ever called from anywhere that doesn't go through that enum validation
